topic: Embedded - Linker Scripts and Memory Maps

turns.content: I'm trying to configure my linker script for a Cortex-M4 using the cortex-m-rt crate, but I keep getting errors about undefined symbols in the heap region. The memory.x file looks correct with FLASH and RAM sections defined, yet when I try to build with cargo, the linker complains that some data from the .bss section isn't being placed correctly.

The issue usually stems from how you define the heap size relative to the actual stack usage in your program. When defining the heap region in memory.x for cortex-m, you must ensure the end address aligns with the maximum stack requirement plus a safety margin. If your .bss section overflows into the reserved stack area or vice versa, the linker will fail because it cannot distinguish between valid data and executable code regions. Specifically, if you use the wrong alignment constraints on your heap definition, the resulting layout might violate the Cortex-M4 memory map requirements, causing undefined behavior at runtime.

That sounds plausible, but I've already added a 128-byte padding between my stack and heap in memory.x. The linker still fails with an error about overlapping regions. Is it possible that the way I'm defining the .data section is conflicting with how rustc places it? Maybe I should try using a different linker script format instead of the standard one provided by cortex-m-rt?

The problem isn't necessarily the format, but rather how you've placed the sections relative to the memory boundaries. In Rust, when you use extern crate cortex_m_rt or link against it, the compiler generates specific section symbols like .text, .data, and .bss that must land exactly where your linker script expects them. If your memory.x defines a FLASH region ending at 0x1000 but your .text section is placed beyond that boundary due to incorrect load addresses or start offsets, you'll get this overlap error regardless of the format used. The cortex-m-rt crate assumes standard conventions for these regions, so deviating from them without updating all references will break compilation.

I understand about the boundaries now, but I'm still confused why simply increasing the RAM size in memory.x isn't solving it even though my variables seem small. Is there a specific constraint on how large the heap can be relative to the stack? Also, does the linker script need to explicitly mention the vector table address for this to work properly?

Yes, there is a strict constraint: the total size of your stack plus heap must not exceed the available RAM region defined in memory.x. If you allocate more RAM than what's physically present or if your code allocates variables that span across section boundaries incorrectly, the linker will reject it. Additionally, while the vector table address isn't explicitly required in every case, the cortex-m-rt crate expects the .text section to start at a specific offset within FLASH. If you define your FLASH region starting at an incorrect address or misalign it improperly with the vector table location (typically 0x1F00 for Cortex-M4), the runtime initialization will fail because the CPU won't know where to jump during reset.

Okay, so I should align my FLASH start address properly and ensure my stack+heap fits within RAM. But what if I need dynamic allocation? Can I just use the standard Rust heap and let the linker handle it automatically without modifying memory.x at all?

You cannot rely solely on automatic handling for embedded systems because the standard Rust heap doesn't account for flash-mapped code or strict memory isolation requirements. While you can define a heap region in your linker script using macros like `__heap_start` and `__heap_limit`, simply letting cargo manage everything without configuring memory.x will result in incorrect symbol placement if your binary exceeds default assumptions. For example, if your program generates more .data than the default RAM allows, or if you need to place specific sections at custom addresses for flash-mapped code (like ROM-resident constants), manual configuration via a properly formatted linker script is mandatory.

topic: Embedded - Linker Scripts and Memory Maps

turns.content: I've read about memory.x for Cortex-M but I still don't get why the linker script is so critical when writing cortex-m-rt code. It seems like just defining sections isn't enough, right?

You're right that it's more than just defining sections. Think of the linker script as a blueprint for physical memory, not just logical data placement. When you define regions in memory.x, you are telling the build system where to put your FLASH and RAM sections relative to the actual silicon layout. If your heap region definition is wrong, the runtime might try to allocate more stack space than physically available on your microcontroller's RAM, leading to hard crashes that are impossible to debug without this script.
